			    CLUB RATES
   Determined that nothing shall be wanting on the part
of the Publishers, to increase the circulation of the
EXPONENT and aid in promoting the objects contem-
plated in its publication, by making it truly an expo-
nent of the views of the women of Utah, sustained by
them, read by them, and its columns enriched with a 
widely circulated expression of their thoughts, we offer
the following liberal terms to clubs for the present vol-
ume:
10 copies; 1 year with a copy to the getter up of
	the club.         -            -         -        -         -      $18.00
15 copies, 1 year, with a copy to the getter up of 
       the club.           -           -         -        -         -      26.00
20 copies, 1 year, with a copy to the getter up of
	the club, and a framed steal engraving 12x 16
	inches, of President Brigham Young, selling  
	price, $2.50      -         -          -          -              34.00
30 copies, 1 year, with a copy to the getter up of
	the club, and a superior first proof steel en-
	graving of President Brigham Young, hand-
	somely framed, selling price $4        -        -    48.00
    Clubs can be commenced at any time, and additions
can be made after the first list of names has been sent.
Getters up of the clubs should notify us of their intentions
so that we can credit them with all names sent in. 
     In all cases give the name and residence of each sub-
scriber, plainly and correctly written, with the time
when each subscription is to commence, and whether for
a whole or half year.
	Money can be remitted by registered letter at the risk
of the publishers.
	Subscribers desiring the address changed from one
Post Office to another should send us their former, as
well as their new address.
	We hope that agents and ladies throughout the Terri-
tory, especially Presidents and Officers of Relief Socie-
ties will aid us by getting up clubs, by bringing the EX-
PONENT favorably to the notice of non-subscribers and
otherwise using their influence to increase its circula-
tion and usefulness.


Woman's Exponent.

VOL. 3.				SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JUNE 15, 1874.					NO. 2.﻿


﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.

   THE NATIONAL congress of brewers pro-
test against the doings of the temperance
movers, which attempt to prove that malt
liquors produce immortality, pauperism and
crime; and they pledge united effort to in-
fluence public opinion in favor of the same,
as wholesome, nutritious and necessary.
They direct a lengthy address to the Presi-
dent and people of the United States, in
which they denounce the movement of the
crusaders as being merely an effort of de-
signing men for personal agrandizement.
The crusaders are evidently waking up
some indivdiuals. Let them proceed.
   REV. DR. John Hall of New York speaks
of the crusade of women in Ohio and else-
where as probably, similar in its effects to
the temperance movement of Father Mat-
hews which some years ago, swept like a
tidal wave over Ireland and portions of Great
Britain and America, and for the time be-
ing, greatly limited drunkenness; and thou-
sands of living, prosperous men, date their
prosperity from that movement. “As a
means of suppressing drinking," says the
Rev. Dr,. “you may call it a failure; as a
means of unspeakable good to all these, you
must call it a success.” There is no doubt
but he is correct in supposing that the pres-
ent temperance movement will also be a
means of salvation to many, though it may
not affect all.
   THERE ARE ninety-nine newspapers pub-
lished in Michigan. Of these, seventy-six
have declared in favor of woman suffrage.
The other twenty-three will doubtless soon
be ashamed to appear against it, and the
woman’s cause will have triumphed in that
State.


[For the EXPONENT

			 RUFFLES.

	La, me! Charlotte, read that again for
grandmother, do. I wern’t noticing till I
I heard you speak about the ruffles.”
     Charlotte read from EXPONENT No. 22,
Vol. 2. “What could look prettier or more
modest, than a pretty, small, trousered foot,
in silk stockings and a neat sandled shoe,
appearing under the skirt of a muslin dress
—the ankles buried as it were in several
rows of lace frills or flounces of fine cam-
bric,” &c.
     “Well I declare,” said grandma, “I never
thought that these old eyes of mine would
ever see the day when the good old fashion
of ruffles would come again. Why there’s
your own mother, child, even before she
could see the daylight, her little head was
completely hid in a heavily wrought cap
with loves of ruffles. Why you wouldn’t
hardly know it was a baby’s head; only
you’d know of course for every body fixed
them so.”
     “But grandmother, the writer of this ar-
ticle is referring to a lady’s under dress, her
pants, you know," observed Charlotte.
“Well, what is the difference, a ruffle’s a
ruffle arn’t it?”
     “Yes, but only think of the almost end-
less task of stitching, and then the outlay of
labor and patience required in this hot,


dusty weather, grandmother, to keep them
neat and in good taste.”
     “I know child, this horrid country isn’t
like old Vermont; and then the Canadians
were glad to help their better off neighbors,
and they were mighty handy too. Your
mother, child, was always rather delicate
when she was young, and she’s had her
white dress ironed often by a young French-
man, who seemed to think it a perfect plea-
sure to work for the Squire’s daughter; and
does not the good book say that the sons of
strangers shall serve you?”
     “Yes grandmother, but if we could bring
ourselves, or others to such torturing toil,
would it not be wrong? Are we not instruc-
ted to practice retrenchment in excessive
labor as well as in expense of material and
time? Indeed, grandmother, is not re-
trenchment in these things, the will of Our
Heavenly Father as expressed by His ser-
vants, the Prophets, unto us?”
     “Well, well, child! the Lord forbid that
your grandmother should ever speak to your
quick ears a word against the blessed Pro-
phets; but, alack a me! arn’t the children
of this generation wise? But why should
our own EXPONENT contain an article of that
kind, telling us about the pretty appearance
of ruffles and such things, if——”
     “Why, grandma, it is merely a ‘clipping’
from another paper, inserted in ours by way
of variety, I suppose, simply to show us how
women dress on the Continent, among the
French, Spanish and Russians.”
						AZURETTA.
     Washington, May 18th, 1874.


[For the EXPONENT.]

			FAULT FINDING.

	The old adage runs in this wise, “Speak
of one’s faults before his face, his virtues
behind his back;” but to this I cannot agree.
	In reproving a friend, we should act
cautiously and gently, not to offend or
wound, and it always has a better effect
when the reproof is given privately. How
bitterly people may be wounded by having
their faults paraded before others, and their
peculiar weaknesses betrayed by, perhaps,
some loved one. I have seen children of
sensitive natures droop under this unneces-
sary punishment. I remember when our
Indian boy was quite a little one, I happened
to speak of a bad habit he had, to a neigh-
bor, not thinking he would notice me.
Looking around I saw his eyes full of tears
and his poor little lip quivering. My
heart condemned me in a moment, and that
was a good lesson to me.
	In watching my children, and trying to
cultivate the good traits of character and
check the evil, as manifested so diversely
in their natures, I have always found it
best, to encourage them to think themselves
capable of doing good; and to lean towards
the good side—the “bright side of the pic-
ture,” rather than to be always ready to
portray its defects. Here is an old maxim
which I think is nearer right, and will
work much better than the one quoted
above:
		"Be to his faults a little blind,
		And to his virtue very kind ”
June 3rd, 1874.			    Anne Niobe.


﻿FLOWERS.

	TIME is the old Justice that examines all
offenders.
	BE not afraid of hard study; it is the
price of learning.
	THE flower of youth never appears more
beautiful than when it bends towards the
Sun of Righteousness.
	GOLDEN WORDS.—In private, watch your
thoughts; in the family, watch your temper;
in company, watch your tongue.
	THE darkest day in any man’s earthly ca-
reer is that wherein he fancies that there is
some easier way of gaining a dollar than by
squarely earning it.
	A FRIEND should be one in whose under-
standing and virtue we can equally confide,
and whose opinion wo can value at once for
its justness and sincerity.
	MENTAL grace is essential to true beauty.
Without it, the fairest form disappoints and
wearies. It is the radiance that sets off
every other charm, and sheds on each its
appropriate hue.
	BY reading we enjoy the dead; by con-
versation, the living; and by contemplation,
ourselves. Reading enriches the memory;
conversation polishes the wit; and contem-
plation improves the judgment.
	THE man who can make his own fire,
black his own boots, carry his own wood,
hoe his own garden, pay his own debts, and
lives without wine and tobacco, need ask no
favor of him who rides in a coach and four.
	AMONG all other virtues, humility, the
loveliest, is pre-eminent. It is the safest
because it is always at anchor; and the man
may truly be said to live the most content
in his calling that strives to live within the
compass of it.
	THE true American patriot, is the “sober
man, anxious in thought, faithful in service,
alive to the least and humblest duty as man
or citizen, sturdy and steady under dull
routine, and resolved that through him the
republic receive no harm.”—J. F. W. Ware.
	OFTENTIMES nothing but adversity will
do for us. We need to be stripped of every
earthly portion, that we may seek entirely
our portion in Jehovah himself. We need
to be turned out of our home on earth, that
we may seek a home in heaven.
	A MAN takes contradiction and advice
much more easily than most people think,
only he will not take it when violently
given, even though it be well founded.
Hearts are flowers; they remain open to the
softly falling dew, but shut up in the violent
downpour of rain.
	OVER and above every other social trait
stands cheerfulness. What the sun is to na-
ture—what God is to the stricken heart
which knows how to lean upon Him—are
cheerful persons in the house and by the
wayside. They go unobtrusively, uncon-
sciously, about their silent mission, bright-
ening up society around them with the hap-
piness beaming from their faces. We love
to sit near-them; we love the expression of
their eye, the tone of their voice. Little
children soon find them out.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT


For the EXPONENT.
			WOMAN ARISE!

	Oh woman, arise! this glow in the skies
	Betokens the advent of morning;
	Thy spirit should smile, exulting the while
	In the day which has published its warning.

	Its glory shall shine in splendor divine
	Thy path, so long dark, to illumine,
	And render thee strong to wrestle with wrong
	Which, crushing thee, crushes all human.

	Thy desolate cry through ages gone by
	To the ear of thy God has ascended;
	He bids thee prepare thy armor to bear
	For soon shall thy penance be ended.

	Thy folly lay by, bid vanity fly,
	And trifling and cowardice follow;
	On purposes high, with calmly fixed eye,
	Disdain pleasures blandishments hollow.

	In God be thy trust, His flat is just;
	He hea[-]s all thy passionate pleading;
	Still cling to his hand, He‘ll teach thee to stand
	Upholding, chastising and leading.

	By ignorance bound by custom enwound
	In meshes not easily broken,
	With talents suppressed where brightest and best,
	Thy intellect seldom has spoken.

	Thy heart true and warm, in every form
	Have pain and pale sorrow at ended;
	And grim-visaged wrong has trampled thee long,
	But soon shall its triumph be ended.

	Full soon shalt thou stand co-equal with man,
	[-]ntrammeled, yet faithful and lowly,
	Triumphantly meek, resistless though weak,
	A conqueror, guiless and holy.

	When no more a slave be noble and brave
	But keep thy heart holy and tender;
	All attributes sweet be wary to keep
	Nor yield them to grandeur and splendor. 

	Thy champions, though few, are valiant and true
	And swiftly their numbers are swelling;
	Th[-]n never despair for lo! in the air
	Pure light is the darkness dispelling.

	Undoubtingly hear this anthem of cheer,
	The voice of a prophet is singing;
	Oh sister rejoice! lift up thy glad voice!
	The bells of thy freedom are ringing!
									LU DALTON.


R. S. REPORTS.

	SALT LAKE CITY, May 26th, 1874.
  A special meeting was held by the Relief 
Society of the 3rd Ward, May 16th, 1874.
There were present as visitors, Miss E. R.
Snow, Mrs. M. I. Horne, Mrs. Zina D., Mar-
garet P. and Susan S. Young, and Mrs. Pre-
cinda Kimball. Mrs Horne spoke encour-
agingly, told the sisters to go on in the
good work they were engaged in, &c.
Miss Snow gave some excellent advice to
mothers in regard to the rearing of their
children and the watchful care they should
have over their daughters; she wished to
impress upon the minds of the young ladies,
the necessity of attending their meetings,
praying for the gifts of God, which it was
their privilege to enjoy; to have a strong
will, and take a firm stand, that they would
perform any duty that was required of them.
She also spoke upon the United Order, its
advantages and blessings. The other sis-
ters above mentioned made some excellent
and appropriate remarks, and were followed
by Bishop Weiler, Counselor John Wayman
and Brother Peter Sinclair. It is a unani-
mous feeling in the Society that they will
try to carry out the instructions that were
given, that they may receive the blessings
which are in store for the faithful.
		Mrs. Zurviah G. Eardly, Sec'y.

				NEPHI, May 27th, 1874.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:—
	Our Young Ladies' Society was organized
March 19th, 1874. Miss Charlotte Hyder
Evans was chosen President, Miss Alice
Evans and Miss Matilda E. Picton Counsel-
ors, Miss Roxey Lucina Bigler Secretary.
Our meetings are pretty well attended and
those who attend feel well in the work. In
our meetings we bear testimony to the truth
of the Gospel and read pieces from the ever
welcome EXPONENT. Miss Alice Evans
has formed a class for all those who wish to
learn the different stitches of netting. We
all take our own work, whatever it may be,
and altogether we have very good and in-
teresting times. Some of the brethren and
elder sisters visit us sometimes and give us
instructions. We hold meetings every oth-
er Friday. Hoping your paper will increase
in circulation and do much toward building
up the Kingdom of God, we remain your
sisters in the Gospel of Christ.
	MISS CHARLOTTE H EVANS, President,
	MISS ROXEY LUCINA BIGLER, Secretary.

	CEDAR CITY, IRON CO., May 19th, 1874.
	Our Relief Society is prospering; liberal
donations have been made to the building
of the St. George Temple; also to gathering
the poor. The sisters generally are self-
sustaining, very few cases of real necessity
have been known, and those have been at-
tended to. We had an excellent meeting
last Thursday, some good remarks from
John M. Higby, Thomas Thorley and Yens
Neilson—the Presidency of the United Order
in this place—followed by a short address
from Brother J. P. Jones; the chief subject
being, classification of labor. A good feel-
ing prevails among the Saints and all seem
willing to do their part.
	With kind regards, I remain your sister,
					MARY ANNE LUNT.

	MENDON, CACHE CO., June 5th, 1874.
DEAR EDITOR:—
	We held our Annual Meeting May 28th.
There were present on the stand, Brothers
Anderson, Donelson, Thumway, Baker and
Gardener. President Willie and Counselors.
Meeting opened in the usual manner with
singing and prayer. Minutes of the previ-
ous meeting were read; also the financial
report of the past year; which was very
satisfactory. Sister Maria Baker spoke on
the necessity of the young ladies being or-
ganized in a class, to work in connection
with the society. We also received good
words of instruction from the brethern that
were present. President Mrs. Willie also
gave some very valuable counsel and thanked
the brethren for their visit. Sisters Baker
and Hancock bore testimony to the truth of
the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Meeting closed
with prayer by Brother Anderson. Wish-
ing the success of every good enterprise,
including the WOMAN'S EXPONENT, we are
respectfully, 
	  ELIZABETH WILLIE, President,
			ANNIE HUGHES, Secretary.

	Secretary Jane Bailey, reports from the 
Relief Society of Moroni, Sanpete Co.,
"The Sisters are united in seeking to do
good; the poor among us are provided for;
several good heavy bed quilts with other
suitable clothing for winter, we have been
able to furnish for those who were in need
of them. The sick also have been cared
for, and the best interest of all looked after,
so far as the influence and power of the
Relief Society extends. And we thank 
God that we are numbered among His free
people, and that these humble yet good
works are alotted unto us to perform. For
one, I would not exchange my position in
my lowly mountain home for all that the
world calls grand and great. I KNOW that I
am FREE here, that the people and the wo-
men of the people of God are free; and what
doth it matter if the world will not see it?
		'And when some trying hour appear,
	  I'll not be over awed,
		But let the scoffing sinner hear
	  That I can speak of God.'"

	The Secretary of the Manti Relief Society,
A. L. Cox, writes:—Our Society was organ-
ized May 11th, 1874. It has accomplished
much good, gaining "little by little." At
the close of the last year, our surplus funds
were used to assist the emigration of the
poor Saints. We hold our regular meetings
on the afternoon of every Fast-day, and
special meetings when business requires.
Our President Mrs. Ann Logan is a woman
of much experience and ability, and the
prevailing sentiment with the Sisters is to
sustain her, and assist in the upbuilding of
Zion. The EXPONENT is a welcome visitor
both in our meetings and at our homes.
Please accept my hearty GOOD wishes for
your success as the Pioneer of female jour-
nalism in Utah.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

				OXFORD, May 3rd, 1874.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	DEAR SISTER:—I have been a reader of
this little paper from its beginning, and I 
think there is none other of its size that can 
compare with it.  To me its precepts are of
great importance; and when I read, I feel a
union in spirit with those of my Sisters,
who are engaged in so good a cause.  All
the principles of this Gospel, so far as I un-
derstand them, afford happiness, in this 
life, setting aside what I expect to receive
in the next, according to my faithfulness.
In my heart I can say to my sisters, let us
not withhold our mites, but let us continue
in well doing.			LITTLE ONE.

			MANTI, May 31st, 1874.
DEAR EXPONENT:—
	Judging from appearances, one might
think that but few, comparatively, really
understand and appreciate the blessings
which surround us.  We not only possess
the right of suffrage, a privilege still denied
to the great majority of our countrywomen,
but we also enjoy a portion of the rights
guaranteed by the Constitution, freedom of
speech and of the press, notwithstanding
the attempts of dishonest officials to deprive
us of them.  And so far from being what
we have been represented by sensationed
newspaper writers,—"The poor, oppressed
and downtrodden women of Utah," we are
quite the reverse; and to-day are enjoying
more freedom than our sisters east or west
of us.  If our sex is destined to become a 
power in the land, what should prevent the
women of Utah being in the front rank?
Certainly not a lack of inclination or ability.
Time and opportunity are all that is required
to call forth the genius and talents that exist
among us.  Lest I encroach too far upon 
your time and space, and weary you or your 
readers;  I will conclude for the present,
hoping that you will still continue to be a 
bold champion for the cause of truth. Very
Respectfully,				A. L. Cox.


Mrs. S. A. Grifford, President of the Praying
Woman's Temperance Union of Worcester, has 
published a card saying that she entered into the
temperance movement with the distinct avowal
that while she regarded labor in the saloons as im-
portant, she did not think it was the first to claim
the efforts of the women, and could not feel it her
duty to lead the bands.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


INTERESTING EXTRACTS,

	From a discourse by President George A.
Smith, delivered May 10th, 1874, in the
New Tabernacle, S. L. City.
	Reported by D. W. Evans for the Des-
eret News.

		 HOME PUBLICATIONS.
     I also advise that the “Juvenile Instruc-
tor” be circulated extensively among our chil-
dren. It is a work calculated to inform
their minds on the principles of the gospel;
from its pages they may also gain a know-
ledge of the history of the Church, as well
as a variety of other useful and entertain-
ing information. It is a very useful publi-
cation, and the benefits it is capable of con-
ferring upon our young people are numer-
ous and great. While speaking on this sub-
ject I will refer to other papers published by
our brethren in these valleys—The “Deseret
News,”' the “Herald,” “Junction,” “Provo
Times,” and the “Beaver and St George En-
terprises,” all of which contain a good deal of
information about our home affairs specially,
and of events in the world generally. I hope
that, in all the stakes of Zion, the people will
manifest a spirit and determination to sup-
port papers which are published for their
benefit. The “Deseret News,” daily, semi-
weekly, and weekly, besides the general
news of the world, also contains many of
the sermons of President Young and others
of the church authorities, and it should be
widely circulated in all the settlements of
the Saints. The mails now run to all parts
of the Territory, and though we can not
boast a great deal about the punctuality of
some of them, yet in nearly every settle-
ment a mail comes along once in awhile
bringing the “Deseret News,” and a man is
pretty safe on the main thoroughfares, in
taking the Weekly, and in many localities
the Semi-weekly, or Daily may be ventured
upon.
	SUSTAINING THE EXPONENT.
We must do something more in relation
to printing. The Woman’s Relief Society
are publishing a paper called the WOMAN'S 
EXPONENT, which is a very ably edited
sheet, and one containing a great deal of in-
formation. I am surprised that all the gen-
tlemen in the Territory do not take it. I
invite all the elders, bishops, and presiding
officers in the stakes of Zion, on their re-
turn home, setting the example themselves,
to solicit all their brethren, and especially
the sisters, to become subscribers of this
little sheet, for I am sure that they will be
interested in the instruction and informa-
tion it contains. I will say that we expect
in a short time, through the patronage of
the brethren and sisters, that the ladies will
be able to enlarge this paper, and to extend
its influence far and wide.
		POWER OF THE LADIES.
   It has been my privilege to make visits
to, and to become acquainted with the Ladies’
Relief Societies in many of the settlements
in the Territory, and I am convinced that
great good results from the labors of these
organizations; and I feel certain that unless
the ladies take hold of any movement de-
signed to forward the work of the Lord in
the last days, its progress will be tardy. In
all parts of the world, when nations are at
war, unless the women take an interest in
the matter, the war goes on very heavily.
I am of the opinion that in the next war be-
tween France and Germany, the French will
get the best of it. Not but what I have a
great opinion of German skill, energy and
pluck, but I am satisfied, from traveling
and personal observation, that the women
of France are thoroughly aroused, and that
in the next war between those two nations,
the Prussians will have to fight the women
of France, and then France will be likely
to win.
	ADVICE TO RELIEF SOCIETIES.
     I say to our sisters of the Relief Societies,
be encouraged, meet together and discuss
all questions that are calculated to interest
or benefit the community, as you have the
ability; and as no man can be elected to
office in this Territory without the vote of
the ladies, make yourselves thoroughly ac-
quainted not only with the politics of the
country, but with every principle of local
government that may be advanced, and
then, whatever is calculated to benefit the
people in their private or domestic circles,
you will be enabled to vote intelligently
and to carry it through without difficulty.
	    WOMEN'S INFLUENCE. 
    We spend a great deal of our money in
following vain fashions, and in purchasing a
great many articles that are useless. These 
societies, if they choose, can make their
own fashions, and they can make them ac-
cording to wisdom, and so as to promote
health; a great many of the fashions of the
world are calculated to destroy health. A
hundred questions connected with domestic 
economy—housekeeping, cooking, making
bread and kindred subjects, that are of im-
portance to the stomach, health and longev-
ity of every man and woman in the Terri-
tory may be properly discussed in these Re-
lief Societies, and useful information dis-
seminated. A great many of the women
in these valleys have not had good oppor-
tunities to become acquainted with the art
of cooking, and that is an art which has
something to do with every person’s hap-
piness. The example of the ladies, and the
influence which they exercise, have a ten-
dency, above all things else, to maintain,
create, and preserve good morals. Men are
apt to behave themselves in the society of
women, and if women act wisely and pru-
dently in guiding and controlling the course
and conduct of each other, they will be able,
to a great extent, to guide, control, and regu-
late the morals and conduct of men. We
think, however, that the policy of the
Christian world, in throwing the responsi-
bility, so far as morality is concerned, en-
tirely upon the heads of women, is a blun-
der; the men should be held responsible for
their own acts, and when they are guilty
of that which is corrupt, low, or degrading,
they should be looked upon as transgressors
and cast aside until, by repentance and up-
rightness, they prove that they are worthy
of confidence. 			
   EXERCISING THE ELCTIVE FRANCHISE.
   Never let an election go by, or any other
occasion in which it is important for us to
take a part, without paying attention to it.
This advice is for the ladies as well as for
the gentlemen, for every lady of twcnty-
one years of age, who is a citizen of the
United States, or whose husband or father
is a citizen of the United States, has a right,
under the laws of Utah, to vote; and no one
need hope to hold office in Utah if the ladies
say no.


"How shall we settle the labor question?"
exclaimed a member of the Georgia Legis-
lature, in the midst of his speech. "By all
going to work, and earning your living hon-
stly!" thundered an irreverent spectator
in the gallery.


PEN AND SCISSORS.
	PLACARDS on the St. Louis street cars de-
clare that "this car can't wait for ladies to
kiss good-bye."
	BULWER considered journalism more
fitted to destroy bad governments than to
construct foundations for good ones,
	WE think there is much truth in the ob-
servation of Josh Billings that "success
don't konsist in never making blunders, but
in never making the same one the seck-
ond time."
	HAPPINESS between husband and wife
can only be secured by that constant ten-
derness and care of parties for each other
which are based upon warm and demonstra-
tive love.  In a word, ever-present and ever-
demonstrative gentleness must reign, or
else the heart starves.
	A WORTHY Quaker thus wrote:—"I ex-
pect to pass through this world  once. If,
therefore, there be any kindness I can show,
or any good thing I can do to my fellow hu-
man beings, let me do it now.  Let me not 
defer nor neglect it, for I will  not pass this
way again."
	AN unexptected meeting of a long separa-
ted father and son took place in a San 
Francisco restaurant the  other day.  The 
father had been in the mines for years, but
through the removal of his family his let-
ters had failed to reach them, and they had
believed him dead until the son recognized
him in the heavily bearded gentleman sit-
ting opposite him at the table.
	SOMEBODY was once offering advice to
General Jackson, during his Presidential ca-
reer, and showing a vast amount of wisdom
about something that did not in the least
concern him.
	He finished at last, as all things earthly
must have an end—when the old General 
said quietly:
	"I knew a man, down in Tennessee, who 
was very poor and had a large family; but
he brought them up well, and showed them
all how to make their fortunes.  How do
you think he did it?"
	"Don't know, I am sure."
	"Taught them to mind their own busi-
ness," replied the old warrior.—Phil. Home
Weekly.
	EXPRESSION IN DR[---]—Owen Meredith 
remarks: "Women are more like flowers
than we think.  In their dress and adorn-
ment they express their natures as the flow-
ers do their petals and colors.  Some wo-
men are like modest daisies and violets--
they never look nor feel better than when
dressed in a plain morning wrapper.  Others
are not themselves unless they can flame out
in gorgeous dyes, like [---] tulip or blush 
rose.  Who has not seen women just like
white lilies?  We know several marigolds
and popies. There are women fit only for
velvets, like dahlias; others are graceful
and airy, like the azaleas.  Now and then
you see hollyhocks and sunflowers.  When
women are free to dress as they like uncon-
trolled by others, and not limited by their
circumstances, they do not fail to express
their true character, and dress becomes a 
form of expression very genuine and useful."


A YOUNG gentleman asked a young lady
what she thought of the marriage state in
general. “Not knowing, can’t tell,” was
the reply, “but if you and I were to put our-
heads together, I could soon give you a defi-
nite answer.”

		SALT LAKE CITY, June 15, 1874.


SPLINTERS.
			   LOCAL.
	Currants were ripe in St. George on the
5th inst.......Dr. S. E. Crain committed sui-
cide in the city jail, on the 9th inst. He
was sentenced to answer for an alleged
crime, and was unable to get bail.......The
proprietors of the Salt Lake Theatre have
had a "benefit" week, the performers playing
without charge for that length of time......
Splendid home raised strawbearies have
been in market for the past ten days.
			GENERAL.
	A Jeffersonville, Indiana saloon keeper
lately blew his own brains out; but he first
killed his baby and  is believed mortally
wounded his wife......A Washington lady
believes Grant would take anything you
would give him, even if it were the small-
pox.......Ingalls has introduced a bill in
Washington, to have the Board of Indian
Commissioners abolished......One hundred
millions of Postal cards have been issued in
this country during the short time they
have been in use.......A new comet, only
visable through a telescope, has been lately
discovered........The House committee on
Territories, at Washington, have agreed to
report a bill defining the qualifications of
deligates from Teritories, which provides
that a man must be twenty-five years of age,
a resident for seven years of the Territory
he represents, and neither a bigamist nor
polygamist......A temperance crusade is in
active progress on the Sandwich Islands,
and a bill has been introduced in the Hawai-
ian cabinet, to prohibit the sale and manu-
facture of intoxicating drinks.....Philadel-
phia is credited with having built the heavi-
est carriage on record. It is styled by the
manufacturing company, the Portable Ho-
tel.


THE DUCHESS of Edinburgh is to be called 
"Her Royal Imperial" or "Imperial Royal
Highness," and will rank next to the Prin-
cess of Wales save on occasions when the
Princess Royal, the Crown Princess of Rus-
sia visits England. This is not in accor-
dance with the rule hitherto followed, by
which the daughters of the sovereign
ranked above the daughters-in-law. But
the Duchess of Edinburgh is imperial by
birth as well as royal by marriage, and for
this is the higher possition assigned her.


For the Exponent.
			WHAT IS LIFE?
	All matter is either organic or inorganic.
We call those organic bodies which are made
up of parts, which, however diversified in
themselves, are mutually adapted and mu-
tually dependent, and each of them capable 
of performing a certain function, or action.
A wax flower has its various parts, mutually
adapted and mutually dependent, but neith-
er in their individual or combined state can
these parts perform any vital function. They
have no principle of life, and without vitali-
ty there can be no organization, since all or-
ganization implies a living arrangement of
elements. The sculptor may cut a block of
marble into the form and fashion of a man;
but when finished, it is after all, only inert
matter in an elegant form. In one word,
wherever we find the living principle in
connection with matter, there we have or-
ganization in vital arrangement. In the
absence of this principle we have nothing
but a dead mass. To the question, "What is
life?" we can give back no answer. That 
there is such a thing as life, or vital action,
cannot be denied, and the only thing which


we can say with certainty concerning it, is,
that life is that which admits of develop-
ment. We go out to the garden with a peb-
ble and a flower seed in our hand. We com-
mit them both to the soil, and thus subject
them to the same agencies and influences.
The seed will burst, and germinate, and de-
velop itself into a beautiful and fragrant
flower; while the pebble may lie there for
years on years without undergoing the least
change. This mysterious life-principle 
which enters into all organized matter, is
found in its highest and most perfect form
in the animal economy, and among animals,
first and pre-eminent in Man. My dear
friends mark, learn and inwardly digest
these fundamental points; and you will find
every time you read, something new, some
things you never understood before. As we
are receiving instruction one of another I
hope this small contribution may meet with
approval. Yours truly,
				M. A. E. WATMOUGH.


SUNDAY SCHOOL JUBILEE.
	We are requested to remind the many friends
of the Sunday School Union, that the Jubilee
will take place on the Twenty-fourth of July,
in the New Tabernacle. The time is drawing
near, and all who are desirous of participating
in the Jubilee are respectfully solicited to send
in their names, secure their tickets and their
song books, tune their voices and be prepared
to join in the united song of praise.
	In what way could the "Twenty-fourth,"
for 1874, be better celebrated than by bringing
together multitudes of children to sing praises
to the Great Giver of all good? He has blessed
the earth for His people's sake. He has caused
the desert to blossom, and the field to yield its
fruitful increase. He has protected His people
and has confused their enemies. Should not
the Saints and the children of the Saints unite
in singing His praise and in giving thanks to
Him? We heartily wish the jubilation may
prove in all respects, to all interested, a success.


ANNIVERSARY MEETING.
	Of the California State Woman's Suffrage
			Association.
	The "New North West" of May 22nd, contains
an interesting account of the fifth anniversary
meeting of the Woman's Suffrage Association
of California. During four sessions which were
held, speeches were made, resolutions formed
and correspondence read, all of an animated
and earnest character. The President of the
State Board of Control, Mrs. M. A. Lewis, re-
ported that in the past year, thirty-six meetings
had been held by the Board, and that it had is-
sued and presented four circulars which con-
tained more than ten thousand pages, and had
written two hundred and fifty-one letters, of
which, one hundred and fifteen had been an-
swered. Mrs. Lewis also congratulated the So-
ciety upon the passage of the Educational Bill
by the Legislature, making women eligible to
every position of authority in the school depart-
ment of the State. The Bill referred to had
been passed in response to a lengthy petition,
containing over five thousand signatures which
was forwarded by the Board.
	The women of Utah have occasion to again
thank the California Woman Suffragists for in-
terposing in behalf of their rights which are
still threatened. At the first session of the
anniversary meeting, the committee on Reso-
lutions presented the following, which was read
and adopted.
	Whereas, The Legislative department of
the Territory of Utah several years since prac-
tically demonstrated its appreciation of and
confidence in the principles of political equal-
ity and self-government, as enunciated by the
founders of the American Republic, by confer-
ring the elective franchise upon the women
within its jurisdiction; and,
	Whereas, The general deportment of the
newly enfranchised class in Utah and Wyo-
ming has been so characterized by quiet, order
and respect for public authority as to attract the
attention and challenge the commendation of
high public officials; and,
	Whereas, There are several bills now before
the Federal Congress, embracing those of Sena-
tors Frelinghuysen, Logan and Sargent and
Representative McKee, each of which, while
aiming to suppress polygamy in Utah, provides,
with the exception of that of Mr. Sargent, to
remand the women back to political subjuga-
tion; therefore,
	Resolved, That the California W. S. Associa-
tion, at its Fifth Anniversary, in convention
assembled, on this 29th day of April, 1874, here-
by records its solemn protest against this crafty
effort on the part of the enemies of Human
Rights to induce the Congress of the United 
States to perform the despotic work of disfran-
chising the women of an entire commonwealth,
thereby, under the specious pretense of promo-
ting public morals and womanly purity, pro-
posing to inflict a punishment upon the polyga-
mic wives of Utah to which only felons should
be subjected, while inflicting no punishment
and imposing no restraint upon polygamous
husbands.
	Resolved, That the Board of Control is re-
quested to prepare and forward to Congress a
memorial asking that Body to protect the wo-
men of Utah in the enjoyment of all the rights
of self-government conferred upon them by the
Legislative department of that Territory, and
that a copy of these resolutions be forwarded
to each Senator and Representative in the Na-
tional Congress from the Pacific Coast, who are
earnestly requested to use their influence to de-
feat any bill which abridges or denies the right
of Suffrage to any person or class on account of
sex.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


HOME AFFAIRS.

	CONFERENCE IN St. George, opened on the
5th inst.

	BRO. L. J. Herrick, Bishop of Ogden, arrived
home on the evening of the 14th inst. from his
European mission.

	THE BEAVER "Enterprise," after another
change, is now edited by Mr. David Tyler, and
published by the Beaver Publishing Co.

	THE "MILLENIAL STAR" of May 19th an-
nounces that the second and third companies
of Saints for this year, were appointed to start
for Utah on the 10th and 24th of this month.

	A COMPANY has been incorporated to build a
railroad from this city to Coalville; distant
about forty-five miles. The capital stock has
been fixed at $1,000,000; of this $62,500 has been
subscribed.

	THE PROVO "Times" of June 4th stated that
the ladies of Provo were signing a petition to
the City Council, asking them not to license
more than one person to sell ardent liquors, and
that only for medical purposes.

	A QUANTITY of live oysters were recently
planted at the mouth of the Weber river where
it enters the Great Salt Lake. We hope the
experiment will be a successful one. The oys-
ters were left by Commissioner Stone, while on
his way West, and planted by Albert P. Rock-
wood, Esq., who takes great interest in fishcul-
ture.

	A GRAND Jubilee for the Sabbath Schools of
Utah County was held at Provo, on Monday,
the 15th inst. The weather was bright and
pleasant and at 10 a.m. about three thousand
children, with several thousand adults had as-
sembled at the Bowery where the jubilee was
to take place. The exercises began with
"Opening Hymn," full chorus, and prayer;
then followed original, and select songs, chiefly
culled from the "Juvenile Instructor" and the
"Charm,"—original recitations by members of
the different Sunday schools, and excellent
music from the brass, and string bands in at-
tendance. The exercises were interspersed with
brief, encouraging addresses by Presidents B.
Young, G. A. Smith, Elders G. Goddard, E.
Snow, J. B. Maiben, and recitation by Elder
D. McKenzie. The children were gratified,
during the recess at midday, with an interior
view of the large woolen Factory, which, by
courtesy of Pres. A. O. Smoot, was thrown open
for that purpose.
	The committee of arrangements were Messers
D. John, W. Paxman, W. N. Dusenberry, A.
Jones and S. S. Jones. Prof. J. E. Daniels con
ducted the musical exercises. Altogether the
accasion passed off pleasantly and the Jubilee
was considered a great success.


LETTER.
	In connection with other interesting cor-
respondence, the following letter was read
before the late anniversary meeting of the
California Woman's Suffrage Association.
	Mrs. Mary J. Collins—Dear Lady:—
Through the politeness of Mr. C. W. Tap-
pan, we have perused the letter of the 4th
inst. from you to his address, in which an


invitation is extended to Woman Suffragists
of Salt Lake to either attend your annual
meeting or address a letter for the occasion;
for which please accept our thanks.
	The receipt of the note being at a late
hour (yesterday evening), affording too little
time consistent for attendance at your Con-
vention, we are happy to address you, al-
though hastily.
	Realizing as we do, that the women of
Utah are highly privileged, we are not in-
different to the interests of our sisters
abroad, and would be most happy in assist-
ing them in their wise and laudable efforts.
For their encouragement we can truly say
that, with us, so far, the franchise is a suc-
cess—refuting many of the objections
against it by its opponents.
	It is presumable that our then acting Gov-
ernor, when signing the bill conferring Wo-
man Suffrage in Utah, felt a little tremulous
—it certainly was a bold step on almost un-
trodden ground, as we were preceded by
neither State nor Territory for example, ex-
cept Wyoming; but, having seen the work-
ing of our ballots, he pronounces it, as well
he may, "the proudest act of his life." The
unaccountable ignorance of people abroad
concerning the state of society, and espe-
cially the social position of woman in Utah,
augmented the risk of the Governor in jeop-
ardising his reputation as a Government of-
ficer in this responsible and magnanimous
act.
	In all ages of the world, the introduction
of beneficial principles and practices has to
combat established customs, usages, preju-
dices, and, above all, the ignorance of man-
kind; and in this respect, the generations
do not seem to have so very much improved
since the time of Christ and His apostles,
or since the days of Socrates and Gallileo,
although the mode of warring opposition is
somewhat modified.
	With intense interest we have watched
the movements of the ladies of California
and elsewhere in our behalf, and have here-
tofore publicly acknowledged our apprecia-
tion of their noble, energetic efforts, and
now feel constrained to say that, in oppos-
ing the passage of those unjust and igno-
minious bills which disgrace the American
Congress, you have not only aided us by
your influence, but have done honor to the
cause which you bravely and earnestly ad-
vocate.
	With many kind wishes for your success
in every laudable enterprise, we are your
fellow laborers for the promotion of good,
			Miss E. R. Snow,
			Mrs. L. Greene Richards,
			Mrs. S. M. Kimball,
			Mrs. M. I. Horne.
	Salt Lake City, April 26th, 1874.


THE BRIDGE across the Mississippi river
was crossed by the first train of cars on the
ninth instant. The engineers were greatly
pleased with the solidity of the bridge.
	AT A late election in Portland Oregon,
through the efforts of the women crusaders,
large casks of ice-water were placed at the
various polling places for the benefit of
thirsty voters. There is good sense in all
such practical forms of crusading.
	The Michigan University College paper is con-
ducted by masculine students, we believe, and they
make very complimentary allusions to their femin-
ine competitors; “They pertinaciously keep their
health and strength in a way that is aggravating
and they persist in evincing an ability for close and
continued mental labor which, to the ordinary esti-
mator of woman's brain power, seems like pure
willfulness.”


﻿DIVERSIONS.

	THE best sewing machine attachment—
The engagement of a pretty seamstress.

	HASH is now considered the connecting
line between the animal and vegetable king-
doms.

	LADIES are said to be like bills of ex-
change, because they ought to be settled at
maturity.

	WHICH is the most difficult train to catch?
Answer.—The 12, 50, because it is always
ten to one if you catch it.

	DID the man who plowed the seas, and af-
terward planted his foot upon native soil,
ever harvest his crops?

	ANDREW Jackson was accused of bad
spelling, but John Randolph defended him
by declaring that "a man must be a fool who
could not spell words more ways than one."

	SHODDY invited Ole Bull to dine with
him, and meet a few friends. At the din-
ner, Shoddy hoped Mr. Bull had brought his
violin. Ole quietly replied, "My violin
never dines out."

	"How far is it to Cub Creek?" asked a
traveler of a Dutch woman. "Only shoost
a little vays." "Is it four, six, eight or ten
miles?" impatiently asked the stranger.
"Yas, I dinks it is," serenely replied the un-
moved gatekeeper.

	Two Milesians were standing at the Fair-
mount water-works, watching the big wheels
splashing the water, when one of them re-
marked:—"Mike isn't this a quare country
where they have to grind the water before
they can use it?"

	COCKNEY: I say, Bill, my good fellor, vich
is the way to Epsom?—Countryman: How
did you know that my name was Bill?—
Cockney: Vy, I guessed it.—Countryman:
How did you know that I was a good fellow?
—Cockney: Vy, I guessed it.—Countryman:
Then guess your way to Epsom.

	A LADY returning from an unprofitable
visit to church, declared that "when she saw
the shawls on those Smiths, and then
thought of the things her own poor girls had
to wear, if it wasn't for the consolation of
religion she did not know what she should
do."

	"JOHNNY," said a mother, to her hopeful
son, as she was reading to him of Pocahon-
tas, in the History of Virginia, "do you
know who Pocahontas was?" "Yes mother,"
said the lad, whose thoughts were upon the
mare that lately trotted a half mile in 1:044,
"she was a fast trotter."

	A MAN sought to annoy a boy whom he
found at work by the road-side, and finally
asked him who his father and mother were,
to which the boy replied: "They are the
parents of a son who knows how to practice
the wise precept of King Solomon, by an-
swering a fool according to his folly."

	DR. Willet said that at one time when he
was a connoisseur in bird-stuffing, he used
to criticise other people's bird-stuffing se-
verely. Walking with a gentleman one
day he stopped at a window where a gigan-
tic owl was exhibited. "You see," said the
doctor to his friend, "there is a magnificent
bird utterly ruined by unskillful stuffing.
Notice the mounting! Execrable isn't it?
No living owl ever roosted in that position.
And the eyes are fully a third larger than
any owl ever possessed." At this moment
the stuffed bird raised one foot and solemnly
blinked at his critic, who said but little more
about stuffed birds that afternoon.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


For the EXPONENT.
		THE RELIEF SOCIETY.
We will toil on bravely, through sunshine and shade,
For our Heavenly Father will still lend us aid;
Though lowly our sphere yet our labors of love,
He honored on earth and still favors above.
“I was an hungered, ye fed me,” once kindly he said,
"The poor of my people likewise ye have fed;
As ye gave unto them, unto me ye have given,
Enter into the joy of your Father in heaven.
“The winter winds chilled me, my raiment was old,
Warm clothing ye gave, to protect me from cold;
My blessing was all that to you then was given,
Your treasures were hid in the Kingdom of Heaven.
“I was thirsty, when lo! from the river's pure brink,
Of the crystal fount springing ye brought forth my drink.
“I was sick and in prison, ye came unto me—
"No longer my servants, but friends ye shall be,”
Then why should we linger or cease from our toil?
The Bride is not ready, all lamps have not oil;
And the watchmen in Zion unceasingly cry,
“Prepare ye, make ready, the Bridegroom is nigh.
								S. B. R.
	Logan.


﻿HOUSEHOLD HINTS.
First Element of a Home.—I never saw a gar-
ment too fine for man or maid; there never was a
chair too good for a cobbler or cooper to sit in;
never a house too fine to shelter the human head.
These elements about us, the gorgeous sky, the im-
perial sun, are not too good for the human race.
Elegance fits man. But do we not value these tools
of house-keeping a little more than they are worth,
and sometimes mortgage home for the mahogany
we can bring into it? I would rather eat my din-
ner off the head of a barrel, or dress after the fash-
ion of John the Baptist in the wilderness, or sit on
a block all my life, than consume all myself before
I get home, and take so much pains with the out-
side that the inside was as hollow as an empty nut.
Beauty is a great thing, but beauty of garments,
house and furniture is a very tawdry ornament com-
pared with domestic love. All the elegance in the
world will not make a home, and I would give
more for a spoonful of hearty love than for whole
shiploads of furniture, and all the upholsterers of
the world could gather together.—“Theodore Par-
ker.”
	Why a Child loves Sugar.—The craving of chil-
dren for sweets is well known to be one of the
most imperious of their appetites. It has refer-
ence probably to that careless activity which espe-
cially characterizes the age of childhood. It is
known that sugar renders easier and more perfect
the digestion of the albuminous food upon which
their growth depends. In respect to these offices
it is therefore nearly essential to their well-being.
And yet how strong, for generations, has been the
prejudice against sugar! Under what difficulties,
and in the face of what discouragements and pro-
tests, have our children obtained the luxury!
	Good Biscuit.—One quart of flour, one table-
spoonful baking powder, butter the size of a wal-
nut; use water; don’t mix stiff; bake quick.
	To soften Hard Water.—You can soften the wa-
ter by adding carbonate of soda—washing soda—as
long as a whitish precipitate is formed. Let it
settle and draw off the clear water above. Some-
times simple boiling will render the water after set-
tling fit for washing. 
	Short Cake.—Rub together one quart of flour,
one teaspoonful of salt, and one cup of butter, lard
or beef dripping. Make into a paste with either
sour, or buttermilk, and add half a tablespoonful of
salaratus dissolved in a little warm water. Make
into a soft dough and bake.
	To Get Rid of Large Ants.—I went out in the
garden and procured a handful of green sage leaves;
sprinkled them in and around my sugar box and in
less than 24 hours they had all left; they are no
trouble at all now; it is a sure way to get rid of
large ants. Try it.—A. L. S.


﻿French Rarebit.—Take three ounces of cheese,
cut it in small square pieces, and set it to fry with
a little piece of butter. When your cheese begins
to melt, have three eggs beaten up with salt and
pepper. Pour them upon your cheese. Stir and
roll it all into a sort of puff and take it off. The
whole operation should not take more than one or
two minutes.
   Elastic Varnish.—As an excellent and easily
manufactured varnish for wicker-work, Mr. F.
Rhien recommends the following composition:—One
part of good linseed oil is boiled down to a sirupy
consistency, and mixed with twenty parts of copal.
This mass is then diluted with oil of turpentine to
the proper thickness. The varnish dries easily,
and remains elastic, so as not to crack by the bend-
ing of the basket.


FACTS AND FIGURES.

	EIGHT sets of twins were born in South
Norwalk, Ct., within five weeks.

	ST. Louis is reported to have invested
$175,000 in the Louisville lottery, but didn't
draw a cent.

Church, in Portsmouth, for $450. A new
case was added and it has remained in the
chapel ever since, and is still in good order.

	PERU has lately discovered new deposits
on guano, which will more than pay her
foreign debt of $180,000,000.

	THE world uses 259,000,000 pounds of tea
and 718,000,000 pounds of coffee every year.
China furnishes nearly all the tea, and Bra-
zil nearly all the coffee.

	IT is calculated that in Alameda county,
Cal., $1,000,000 is invested in the manufac-
ture of beet sugar alone. Last year the
yield was about 20,000 tons, from which
about 3,800,000 pounds of saccharine matter
was produced. It is expected that this sea-
son's crop will be much greater.

	MR. Silas Cooper of Lincoln county, West
Virginia, is claimed to be the oldest man in
the State. He was one hundred and six
years old on the 9th of last March. Mr.
Cooper is as quick of foot as many men at
fifty, and able to stand a great deal of exer-
cise. His wife died a few months ago, at 
the age of one hundred and ten.

	ACCORDING to statistics derived from the
"Bureau of Education," it is calculated that
out of the 38,558,371 of the population of
the United States of all ages, 12,135,799, or
31.47 per cent. of the total population,
must be excluded from the number who are
able, through reading and writing, to ex-
change information with others for the
guidance of their conduct.

	THE oldest organ in the United States is
in the chapel of St. John's Church, at Ports-
mouth. It was imported in August,  1713, and
presented by Thomas Brattle to the Queen's
Chapel, in Boston, which after the death of
Queen Anne, was called the King's Cha-
pel. In 1756 the organ was sold to St. Paul's
Church, where it remained until 1836, eighty 
years, when it was again sold to St. John's

	AHMED, an old gentleman living in Tre-
bizoned, Asia Minor, is said to have reached
his 138th year. He enjoys excellent health
and spirits, though he has long survived all
his relatives. From his youth upward he
has been a strong advocate of bodily exer-
cise as a preservative of health, and until
quite lately has been in the habit of taking
long "constitutionals" daily. Ahmed is
not the only instance of remarkable longev-
ity at Trebizoned. Another old man has
just died who had reached his 125th year.


PRACTICAL HINTS.
	MOUNT PLEASANT, SANPETE Co.
						May 3rd, 1874.
EDITOR EXPONENT:—Being desirous to
commit a few of my thoughts to paper, I
forward the same to you, for publication if
you deem it worthy.
	I address myself principally to the young,
although young and old have the same priv-
ileges in relation to the subject of which I
will write. Let those who possess an edu-
cation do their best to impart  knowledge to
others—striving to do so without show or
noise, going quietly and perseveringly
along in the work of developing and im-
proving the human mind—to feed and in-
struct and implant the principles of eternal
truth. The young lady who teaches her
sister the simple arts of reading and writ-
ing is putting in her possession the key to
all written knowledge, and placing within
her reach the power of active thought.
	Let every young lady's first duty be, to im-
prove, educate and elevate herself in the
social scale, helping forward all those asso-
ciated with her, by all reasonable means.
Therefore, young ladies, determine in your
own minds that you will advance, and the
first step is already taken, and half the bat-
tle won; and striving to advance yourselves,
will prove the most effectaul way of stimu-
lating and encouraging others. Example is
better than precept, and your own actions
will teach far more emphatically than words
can do, I speak of this as a duty binding
upon every young lady connected with the
Church and Kingdom of God. There is a
dignity in every effort that is made to culti-
vate talent and improve every gift that
the Almighty has bestowed upon us. It
produces a well regulated mind, it gives vir-
tue the mastery over vice, it drives away
care and secures comfort and will eventu-
ally aid us in being instruments in the
hands of our great Creator to do much good
to ourselves and our fellow creatures. Let
self-reform and self-improvement be our
motto.
	In conclusion, let me beg of the young
sisters to abserve good manners, and pro-
per deportment—at all times and under all
circumstances—and thus ultimately gain
power over every weakness and every evil
that may at any time present itself among
them.
	With a fervent desire to do good, I am,
your sister in the Gospel of Peace.
				MARGARET F. MARRISON.


HYGIENIC NOTES.

	TO REMOVE BOILS.—Dr. Simon, a phy-
sician of Loraine gives a new cure for boils,
by treating them with camphorated alcohol.
As soon as the culmination point of a boil
makes its appearance, he puts a little of the
liquor in a saucer, and dipping the ends of
his little fingers in it, rubs the inflamed sur-
face, especially the central part, repeating
the operation eight or ten times for about
half a minute. He then allows the surface
to dry, placing over it a slight coating of 
camphorated olive oil. He says that four
such applications will, in almost all cases,
cause boils to dry up and disappear. The
application should be made at morning, noon
and evening.

	EATING BETWEEN MEALS.—In regard to
eating between meals, if persons would eat
only at regular intervals there would be no
craving; but as the habit of eating irregu-
larly is continued, it becomes stronger. I
know it is a common practice for people to


eat as they feel a longing for it. When
children are allowed this privilege, as a gen-
eral rule, it makes them fretful and un-
healthy. When I was a child it was a rare
thing that I asked for anything to eat be-
tween meals; as I grew older I got into the
habit of eating whenever I felt the slight-
est degree of hunger, and was hungry near-
ly all of the time at that; feeling miserable
in the bargain: but, happily for me, I saw an
article in the "Rural" bitterly opposing this
injurious habit, and it so completely disgus-
ted me with it that I made a promise then 
and there never to eat between meals. Since
then I have felt better in both body and
mind and it is seldom that I care about 
breaking my promise; when I do I conquer
the feeling and feel far better than I would
if I had eaten. I speak from experience
having tried both ways.—M. Lang in R.
N.Y.

BENEFITS OF SUNSHINE.—The human
being is like a plant—neither will thrive in
the dark. Nor will either prosper in the
twilight or in the shade. Show me a fam-
ily that lives in a house heavily shaded by
overhanging boughs of trees, so that the
sunshine seldom or never falls upon any side
of it, and I will show you a people who
never enjoy good health. They are not ex-
actly wilted nor bleached; but they have
that bleached look presented by the unfortu-
nate inmates of prisons, and the drooping
appearance seen in plants that make a poor
show of growing in dark, out of the way
places. Vigor is altogether impassible in
the absence of sunshine. I know many
people who think themselves intelligent,
and who are filled with wise saws and sage
proverbs as to the preservation of health,
who live in darkened rooms. Sunshine
fades the carpets and warps the furniture,
they say; but faded carpets are better than
faded cheeks, and warped furniture is better
than crooked spines and bowed leggs and
arms. Flies will get into the house if it is
light, is another excuse for ample window 
window curtains and heavy shade trees—the
latter, in many cases, the poisonous and too 
odorous Chinese Ailanthus.	*	*
Sunshine is grand medicine for the invalid,
and coupled with plenty of fresh air, and
the restorative preparations drawn by the
plants from air and sunshine, it is one of the
finest and safest remedies. It is also one of
the best of the preventives of sickness.
Admit it, then, to every nook and corner of
your dwellings, that it may banish malaria,
and implant strength and vitality into your
minds and bodies. And, speaking of the
benefits of sunshine, we recommend a sun-
shine bath. It costs nothing, but, taken
properly, it yields health, strength and elas-
ticity of spirits.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.				15﻿


ECHO ANSWERS.—Of what has heaven
given us an equal share?—Air.
    What does a rumor often do when it flies?
—Lies.
   Which is the loveliest flower that grows?
—Rose.
    Whose children are we apt to think the
sweetest flowers?—Ours.
    What in manners is sure to please?—Ease.
    What will frequently overcome the most
austere?—Tear.
   What loses its flavor when we borrow it?
—Wit.
   What is it that wealth seldom extinguish-
es?—Wishes.
   What traits are difficult to exterminate?—
Innate.	
   Mlle. de Rothschild has just passed her exami-
nation as a public instructress.


[FOR THE EXPONENT.
	HARMONY OF COLORS.

	We, as a people, are having many splen-
did opportunities to beautify and embellish
our homes by the workmanship of our own
hands. And in order that we may do so
scientifically, that every addition we make
for improvement, may be in unison with the
furniture, wearing apparel, or other articles
already in use, it is necessary that we should
obtain, by diligent research and study of
the fine arts, a knowledge of how to arrange
and decide for ourselves the kind of colors
we wish to bring together, that the whole
may so blend and beautifully harmonize,
not only in things themselves but with all
their immediate surroundings, as to be pleas-
ing and satisfactory to the trained eye of
the most refined. I would refer in an espe-
cial manner to the selection of colors in the
manufacture, by our ladies, of beautiful bed
quilts, hearth rugs, &c., although these re-
marks apply equally as well to the laying
out of flower gardens, the arrangement of
bouquets, the trimming of hats and bonnets,
painting of rooms, papering of walls or the
adornment of the person; and as we ladies
are going into the printing business exten-
sively, my suggestions would also be appli-
cable in the printing of rainbow colored
cards, bills, and every instance where vari-
ous colors are brought in contact with each
other, so that nothing may be discordant or
displeasing to the observing or criticising
eye. One thing to be remembered is, that,
as to the musician a note inserted in the
wrong place is to his trained ear a discord,
so is the introduction of an inharmonious
color a discord to the eye of the artist. How
often is it the case in our associations to-
gether, that a sister will bring out, for us to
admire, that exquisite bed quilt that has
taken so much of her valuable time, atten-
tion and exertions to put together? But
she has embellished it with all the colors of
the prismatic spectrum, the hues of the
rainbow, with all the properties of the pho-
togenic and sciogenic elements of light and
shade without due regard to the harmony
that should exist in the distribution and ar-
rangement of the variety of colors inserted.
This brings to my mind quite forcibly, the
scene of the colors displayed by the thou-
sands of sisters when seated in our densely
crowded tabernacle. Perhaps in a future
time, I may have something to suggest on
this subject, through your columns, to my
sisters of the Relief Societies. Understand-
ing from experience that it is one thing to
find fault, and quite another thing to mend a
fault I purpose giving a few hints on this
subject, realizing that the diffusion of all
useful knowledge is a principle we, as a peo-
ple believe in; and from whatever source it
may emanate it is acceptable and instruct-
ive. Every one must have observed that
certain colors when brought together, set off
each other to advantage; while others have
altogether a different effect. This must be
carefully attended to by whoever would dis-
play beauty or elegance in her work. Any
one color will appear well on a white ground.
Red comes in between white, black or yel-
low; blue next to white or yellow, green
next to black or white, orange or gold color
must be contiguous to black or brown. Let
me here remark that a white article reflects
all the rays that constitute a white light, al-
though in some instances it may reflect some
colored light also, according as the surfaces
are smooth, rough, glossy or channelled. The
optical effect of a color is greatly modified
by the condition of the surface of the colored
article; thus, pieces of silk, cotton, woolen,
linen and velvet although dyed of exactly
the same hue and tone of color, appear to be
of quite different tints. The depth or in-
tensity of color, presented by velvets and
certain flowers, such as the heartsease, is
due to the surface being channelled, ridged,
or furrowed. Here are a few instances, how
colors can appear to be changed by contact
with other colors. Red, for example, by be-
ing placed in contact with blue, appears yel-
low, in contact with yellow it has a bluish
appearance, with green it appears purer and
brighter, in contact with black it appears
duller, with white it appears lighter and
brighter; gray also makes it appear more
bright. Place two pieces together of dif-
ferent colors but of the same tone or inten-
sity. If they are not too wide, they will
appear quite different from what they do
when viewed separately; this is called con-
trast of color. If we place, side by side,
two pieces of the same color but different
shades, the light one will appear lighter, and
the dark one darker; this is called contrast
of tone. Blue must not be placed by the
side of violet, because blue enters into the
composition of violet; the blue makes the
violet look faded, while the violet gives the
blue a greenish appearance. A deep blue
and a deep red both gain intensity of color
by contact with each other. Yellow and
blue are so dissimilar that their contrast is
sufficiently great to produce a favorable as-
sociation, while red and orange are quite
the reverse. Red and blue harmonize bet-
ter than red and violet; yellow and red har-
monize better than yellow and orange, al-
though these latter will harmonize better
than red and orange, because red predomi-
nates in the orange. For the same reason,
yellow and green appear better than blue
and green. When two light colors do not
look well together, separate them with
white, but the deep colors should be separa-
ted by black. Red and orange do not look
well together, yet if separated by black an
agreeable and harmonious effect is produced.
Much more could be said on this subject. I
know from experience that my sisters, the
ladies of Utah, have in many instances, to
use up whatever pieces they may happen to
have by them; but where we have to dye
any of them, we can have an eye to the har-
mony of colors. We must consider that
pink comes under the denomination of red,
because red enters largely into the composi-
tion of pink; as blue does in violet, yellow
in green and orange. In the prismatic spec-
trum and in the rainbow we look for our
standard colors; of these, there are three,
viz., red, blue and yellow. These are called
primary colors, and when transferred
through a prism upon a white surface, pro-
duce a white light; therefore, either one of
the three should always be omitted and
never placed beside the other two.
	As my communication has already assum-
ed such extensive dimensions, while the
subject in hand is not nearly exhausted, I
will defer further remarks till some future
period.				R. J. FILCE.
	20th Ward, S. L. City.

of Government, in Howard College, at Union
Springs, New York. Mary P. Stockbridge,
the other member of the Law. Class, will remain
in Ann Arbor to study another year.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿THE HABIT OF FRETTING.
	Fretting is both useless and unnecessary;
it does no good and a great deal of harm,
yet it is almost a universal sin. More or less
we are all given to it. We fret over almost
everything. In  summer because it is too
hot, and in  winter because it is too cold; we
fret when it rains because it is wet; when
we are sick, or when anybody else is sick.
In short, if anything or everything dosn’t
go just to suit our particular whims and
fancies, we have one grand general refuge
—to fret over it. I am afraid fretting is
much more common among women than
among men. We may as well own the
truth, my fair sisters, if it isn’t altogether
pleasant. Perhaps it is because the little
worries, and cares and vexations of our daily
life harrass our sensitive nerves more than
the more extended enterprises which gen-
erally take the attention of men. Great
wants develop great resources, but the lit-
tle wants and worries are hardly provided
for, and like the nail which strikes against
the saw, they make not much of a mark,
but they turn the edges terribly. I think if
we looked upon all the little worries of one
day as a great united worry, self-control to
meet it would be developed. But as they
generally come only one or two little things
at a time, they seem so very little that we
give way, and the breach once in the wall
soon grows larger. Now, I don’t believe in
the cant that a woman must always, under
all and any circumstances, wear a smiling
face when her husband comes home, or that
she needs to take her hands out of the dough
or drop the baby on the floor to run and
meet him at the door. But I do believe—
nay. I know, for I have seen it with my own
eyes among my friends—that many a wo-
man has driven a kind husband away from
her, away from his home and its sacred in-
fluences, and caused him to spend his time
at a billiard table or in a drinking saloon,
amid their profane influences, simply by
her ceaseless fretting over trifles which
were not worth a word, much less the peace
and happiness of a home. I know that many
a mother has brought up and developed a
daughter just like herself, who, in her turn,
would wreck and ruin the comfort of an-
other family circle. I know that many a
mother has turned her son against her own
sex, and made him dread and dislike the
society of women, by her example, con-
stantly set before him. And knowing all
this, my sisters—and brothers, too, if they
need it—I know that we ought to set our
faces like flint against this useless, sinful,
peace-destroying and home-disturbing habit
of fretting.


WHO SHOULD NOT BE A WIFE.

	Has that woman a call to be a wife who
thinks more of her silk dress than her chil-
dren, and visits her nursery no oftener than
once a day? Has a woman a call to be a
wife who calls for a cashmere shawl when
her husband's notes are being protested?
Has that woman a call to be a wife who sits
reading the last new novel while her hus-
band stands before the glass vainly trying
to pin together a bottomless shirt bosom?
Has that woman a call to be a wife who ex-
pects her husband to swallow diluted coffee,
soggy bread, smoky tea, and watery pota-
toes six days out of the seven? Has she
the call to be a wife who flirts with every
man she meets, and reserves her frowns
for the home fireside? Has she a call to be
a wife who comes down to breakfast in
abominable curl papers, a soiled dressing-


gown, and shoes down at the heel? Has
she a call to be a wife whose husband's love
weighs naught in the balance with her
next-door neighbor’s damask curtains or
velvet carpet? Has she a call to be a wife
who would take advantage of a moment of
conjugal weakness to extort money or ex-
tract a promise? Has she a call to be a wife
who takes a journey for pleasure, leaving
her husband to toil in a close shop, and have
an eye, when at home, to the servants and
children? Has she a call to be a wife to
whom a good husband’s society is not the
greatest earthly blessings, and a house full
of rosy children its best furnishing and pret-
tiest ornaments?


﻿FROM KANAB.
				May 22nd, 1874.
DEAR EXPONENT:—
	It is very pleasant here now. The wind
has blown a good deal this spring, but for
the past two weeks the weather has been
fine and gardens are looking well.
	Kanab grows slowly, owing to the poverty
of the people. I should not say poverty
either; what I mean is, that we have had so
much to contend with, that it has taken all
the time and means to feed us, without
building much. But I like the place for all
that. We were sent here by those whose
right it is to direct us, and I have never for
one moment felt to murmur. My children
have been deprived of many advantages,
which seems a draw back, but if they only
have the Spirit of God with them and learn
of His ways, they will be all right. So long
as we are in the line of duty, what matters
it?
	The family organization works well here,
so far. The Saints feel well, and all looks
bright. We may be a little short of some
conveniences for a while, until we get into
working order, but I believe we will also be
greatly blest. We may yet have some trou-
ble with the Navajoes, but the Lord is able
to turn away the wrath of the Indians. And
now that the brethren are all on this side of
the river, I feel that all will be well.
	We have good meetings here, and a good
school now, which my children attend. And
I feel very thankful for these, with all the
other blessings. Respectfully, &c.
                                             H. M. B.

minded woman of whom we hear so much is a
myth, since the very best specimens of woman-
hood, both in England and America, are those
identified with the suffrage movement.” In Na-
ples, she met Mary Sommerville, one of the
most celebrated mathematicians of the age, who at
“90 years of age was as bright, cheerful, and as
deeply interested in passing events, as the young-
est of the circle.

	EVERY flower may be supplied with its
favorite soil by the exercise of a little pa-
tience and observation. A light soil suits
nearly all descriptions. Almost any piece
of earth which is thoroughly dug and
dressed yearly, may be made suitable to
the production of flowers. All bulbs, car-
nations, pinks, auriculas &c., love a mixture
of sand.—ENGLISH LADY.
